text:
At length came night, ah! grisly hour,  
Ah! shivering time that stirs the dead,  
When demons mount the sullen cloud’s dim tower,  
—The phantom sat beside my bed.
source_text:
At last came night, ah! horrid hour,
Ah! chilling time that wakes the dead,
When demons ride the clouds that lower,
—The phantom sat upon my bed.

text:
Give me the splendid, silent sun with all his beams full-dazzling,  
Give me autumn fruit, ripe and red, from the orchard,  
Give me a field where the unmown grass is growing,  
Give me an arbor, give me the grape upon the trellis,  
Give me fresh corn and wheat, give me animals moving serenely, teaching  
content,  
Give me nights wholly still as on the high plateaus west of the  
Mississippi, and I looking up at the stars,  
Give me, fragrant at sunrise, a garden of fair flowers where I may  
walk undisturbed,  
Give me for marriage a sweet-breath’d woman of whom I should never weary,  
Give me a perfect child, give me, apart from the world’s clamor, a rural domestic life,  
Give me to sing spontaneous songs, recluse and by myself, for my own ears only,  
Give me solitude, give me Nature, give me again, O Nature, your primal  
sanities!
source_text:
Give me the splendid silent sun with all his beams full-dazzling,
Give me autumnal fruit ripe and red from the orchard,
Give me a field where the unmow’d grass grows,
Give me an arbor, give me the trellis’d grape,
Give me fresh corn and wheat, give me serene-moving animals teaching
content,
Give me nights perfectly quiet as on high plateaus west of the
Mississippi, and I looking up at the stars,
Give me odorous at sunrise a garden of beautiful flowers where I can
walk undisturb’d,
Give me for marriage a sweet-breath’d woman of whom I should never tire,
Give me a perfect child, give me away aside from the noise of the
world a rural domestic life,
Give me to warble spontaneous songs recluse by myself, for my own ears only,
Give me solitude, give me Nature, give me again O Nature your primal
sanities!

text:
All truths abide within all things,
They neither urge their own coming forth nor oppose it,
They need not the surgeon’s obstetric forceps,
The mean is unto me as vast as any,
(What is less or more than a touch?)
source_text:
All truths wait in all things,
They neither hasten their own delivery nor resist it,
They do not need the obstetric forceps of the surgeon,
The insignificant is as big to me as any,
(What is less or more than a touch?)

text:
Let warlocks dour, an’ shrivel’d hags,  
Recount how wi’ you, on ragwort nags,  
They skim the muirs an’ reeling crags,  
Wi’ felon speed;  
And in kirk-yards seal anew their leagues,  
Owre howkit dead.
source_text:
Let warlocks grim, an’ wither’d hags,
Tell how wi’ you, on ragweed nags,
They skim the muirs an’ dizzy crags,
Wi’ wicked speed;
And in kirk-yards renew their leagues,
Owre howkit dead.

text:
Whatever contents the soul is true;
Prudence wholly contents the hunger and the surfeit of souls,
Itself alone at last contents the soul,
The soul bears that boundless pride which turns from every teaching
save its own.
source_text:
Whatever satisfies souls is true;
Prudence entirely satisfies the craving and glut of souls,
Itself only finally satisfies the soul,
The soul has that measureless pride which revolts from every lesson
but its own.

text:
SWELLFOOT:
What! ye who are
Those very beasts that, laid before her altar
With blood and groans, salt-cake, and fat, and entrails,
Still ever appease her reluctant will
When taxes are withheld?
source_text:
SWELLFOOT:
What! ye that are
The very beasts that, offered at her altar
With blood and groans, salt-cake, and fat, and inwards,
Ever propitiate her reluctant will
When taxes are withheld?

text:
THE MOON:
The snow upon my spiritless mountains
Is melted into living fountains,
My steadfast oceans flow, and sing, and shine:
A spirit from my heart breaks forth,
It robes with unlooked-for birth
My cold, bare bosom: Oh! it must be thine
On mine, on mine!
source_text:
THE MOON:
The snow upon my lifeless mountains
Is loosened into living fountains,
My solid oceans flow, and sing and shine:
A spirit from my heart bursts forth,
It clothes with unexpected birth
My cold bare bosom: Oh! it must be thine
On mine, on mine!

text:
Behold, through thee, as base as all the rest,
Through laughter, dancing, feasting, supping, of the crowd,
Within the gowns and trinkets, within those wash’d and tended faces,
Behold a hidden, voiceless loathing and despair.
source_text:
Behold through you as bad as the rest,
Through the laughter, dancing, dining, supping, of people,
Inside of dresses and ornaments, inside of those wash’d and trimm’d faces,
Behold a secret silent loathing and despair.

text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,  
Whom fair-ankled Leda, blent in love  
With mighty Saturn’s heaven-obscuring Child,  
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,  
Bore in gladness: gentle Pollux, free from blame,  
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.  
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save  
And ships, whose swiftness skims along the wave.  
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea  
Are roaring, and the sailors fearfully  
Invoke the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,  
Assembled in affright upon the lofty prow,  
And sacrifice white lambs,—the wind, and then  
The huge wave bursting close behind again,  
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear  
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,  
On yellow wings sweeping athwart the sky,  
And lull the blasts to voiceless tranquility,  
And strew the waves upon the white sea’s bed,  
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread  
The sailors rest, rejoicing at the sight,  
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.
source_text:
Ye wild-eyed Muses, sing the Twins of Jove,
Whom the fair-ankled Leda, mixed in love
With mighty Saturn’s Heaven-obscuring Child,
On Taygetus, that lofty mountain wild,
Brought forth in joy: mild Pollux, void of blame,
And steed-subduing Castor, heirs of fame.
These are the Powers who earth-born mortals save
And ships, whose flight is swift along the wave.
When wintry tempests o’er the savage sea
Are raging, and the sailors tremblingly
Call on the Twins of Jove with prayer and vow,
Gathered in fear upon the lofty prow,
And sacrifice with snow-white lambs,—the wind
And the huge billow bursting close behind,
Even then beneath the weltering waters bear
The staggering ship—they suddenly appear,
On yellow wings rushing athwart the sky,
And lull the blasts in mute tranquillity,
And strew the waves on the white Ocean’s bed,
Fair omen of the voyage; from toil and dread
The sailors rest, rejoicing in the sight,
And plough the quiet sea in safe delight.

text:
I am the bard of the Body and I am the bard of the Soul,
The delights of heaven are with me and the torments of hell are with me,
The first I engraft and magnify upon myself, the latter I render
into a new tongue.
source_text:
I am the poet of the Body and I am the poet of the Soul,
The pleasures of heaven are with me and the pains of hell are with me,
The first I graft and increase upon myself, the latter I translate
into new tongue.

text:
It is well—against such I speak not a word, I am their poet also,
Yet lo! such quickly fade, consumed for religion’s sake,
For not all matter is fuel for heat, the impalpable flame, the essential
life of the earth,
Even as such are not for religion.
source_text:
It is well--against such I say not a word, I am their poet also,
But behold! such swiftly subside, burnt up for religion’s sake,
For not all matter is fuel to heat, impalpable flame, the essential
life of the earth,
Any more than such are to religion.

text:
This was a man who might have made  
Of Hell itself a Heaven bright—and thus, in bliss,  
Have won a Heaven for his own abode;  
Yet he in shadows unperceived confided,—  
And doomed himself to madness.
source_text:
This was a man who might have turned
Hell into Heaven—and so in gladness
A Heaven unto himself have earned;
But he in shadows undiscerned
Trusted.—and damned himself to madness.

text:
Flaunting sunshine, I need not thy basking—lie not above!
Thou lightest but the surfaces; I compel the surfaces and the depths as well.
source_text:
Flaunt of the sunshine I need not your bask--lie over!
You light surfaces only, I force surfaces and depths also.

text:
'T is but an instant's play,  
'T is a dear ambush,  
Only to let Bliss  
Win her own surprise!
source_text:
'T is an instant's play,
'T is a fond ambush,
Just to make bliss
Earn her own surprise!

text:
Wild rages the midnight storm  
O’er the wild mountain,  
Dark clouds the night deform,  
Swift rushes the fountain—
source_text:
Fierce roars the midnight storm
O’er the wild mountain,
Dark clouds the night deform,
Swift rolls the fountain—

text:
To Mercy, Pity, Peace, and Love,  
All do in anguish pray,  
And unto these sweet virtues bright  
Return their thanks alway.
source_text:
To Mercy, Pity, Peace, and Love,
All pray in their distress,
And to these virtues of delight
Return their thankfulness.

text:
Our mournful fall in kirk and state  
Passes all my telling:  
The Whigs cam’ o’er us for a bane,  
An’ now we’re done wi’ thriving.  
Awa’ Whigs, &c.
source_text:
Our sad decay in church and state
Surpasses my descriving:
The Whigs cam’ o’er us for a curse,
An’ we hae done wi’ thriving.
Awa’ Whigs, &c.

text:
If thou shouldst meet a lassie,
In grace and beauty shining,
That e’en thy chosen lassie,
Whom erst thy breast was twining
In warmth, had ne’er such tining;
O that’s the lassie, &c.
source_text:
If thou shalt meet a lassie,
In grace and beauty charming,
That e’en thy chosen lassie,
Erewhile thy breast sae warming,
Had ne’er sic powers alarming;
O that’s the lassie, &c.

text:
The negro grips fast the reins of his four horses, the block swings
beneath on its chain bent over and bound,
The negro who guides the long dray of the stone-yard, steadfast and
tall he stands poiz’d on one leg on the string-piece,
His blue shirt reveals his ample neck and breast and loosens about
his hip-band,
His look is calm and masterful, he flings the droop of his hat
back from his forehead,
The sun lies on his crispy hair and mustache, lies on the black of
his polish’d and flawless limbs.
source_text:
The negro holds firmly the reins of his four horses, the block swags
underneath on its tied-over chain,
The negro that drives the long dray of the stone-yard, steady and
tall he stands pois’d on one leg on the string-piece,
His blue shirt exposes his ample neck and breast and loosens over
his hip-band,
His glance is calm and commanding, he tosses the slouch of his hat
away from his forehead,
The sun falls on his crispy hair and mustache, falls on the black of
his polish’d and perfect limbs.

text:
Sea of outspread ground-swell,  
Sea breathing wide, convulsive breaths,  
Sea of the brine of life and of graves unshoveled, yet ever ready,  
Howler and scooper of storms, capricious and dainty sea,  
I am one with thee, I too am of one phase and of all phases.
source_text:
Sea of stretch’d ground-swells,
Sea breathing broad and convulsive breaths,
Sea of the brine of life and of unshovell’d yet always-ready graves,
Howler and scooper of storms, capricious and dainty sea,
I am integral with you, I too am of one phase and of all phases.

text:
The noon was calm and bright:—about that column  
The vaulting heaven and girdling sea  
Outspread in silence deep and grave and solemn  
The shadow of brief frenzy hurled on me,  
So that I knew not mine own misery:  
The isles and mountains in the day  
Like clouds lay hushed afar; and I could see  
The town among the woods beneath that lay,  
And the dark rocks that bound the bright and glassy bay.
source_text:
The noon was calm and bright:—around that column
The overhanging sky and circling sea
Spread forth in silentness profound and solemn
The darkness of brief frenzy cast on me,
So that I knew not my own misery:
The islands and the mountains in the day
Like clouds reposed afar; and I could see
The town among the woods below that lay,
And the dark rocks which bound the bright and glassy bay.

text:
He led them ranging o’er the sandy way,  
Yet, ever mindful of his cunning art,  
Backward and forward still he made them stray,  
Till tracks that seemed before were found apart;  
Then to the ocean spray he cast his shoe,  
And for each foot he fashioned, stout and light,  
A kind of raft of tamarisk and boughs,  
And bound them fast in one with withy twigs.
source_text:
He drove them wandering o’er the sandy way,
But, being ever mindful of his craft,
Backward and forward drove he them astray,
So that the tracks which seemed before, were aft;
His sandals then he threw to the ocean spray,
And for each foot he wrought a kind of raft
Of tamarisk, and tamarisk-like sprigs,
And bound them in a lump with withy twigs.

text:
“But one was silent; her cheeks and lips were fair,
Shifting their colour like lilies newly blown,
Beneath a bright acacia’s shadowed hair,
That, waved by the wind amid the sunny noon,
Betrayed her soul was trembling; and full soon
That Youth arose, and breathless stood to look
On her and me, as for some speechless boon:
I smiled, and took their both hands within my own,
And felt a gentle joy from what their spirits shook.
source_text:
‘But one was mute; her cheeks and lips most fair,
Changing their hue like lilies newly blown,
Beneath a bright acacia’s shadowy hair,
Waved by the wind amid the sunny noon,
Showed that her soul was quivering; and full soon
That Youth arose, and breathlessly did look
On her and me, as for some speechless boon:
I smiled, and both their hands in mine I took,
And felt a soft delight from what their spirits shook.

text:
...
Seized with a sudden longing for new meat,
He in his sacred crib laid down
The hollow lyre, and from the cavern sweet
Burst forth with mighty leaps to the mountain’s crown,
Turning in his mind some subtle feat
Of pilfering craft, such as a rogue might
Contrive in the solitary season of dark night.
source_text:
...
Seized with a sudden fancy for fresh meat,
He in his sacred crib deposited
The hollow lyre, and from the cavern sweet
Rushed with great leaps up to the mountain’s head,
Revolving in his mind some subtle feat
Of thievish craft, such as a swindler might
Devise in the lone season of dun night.

text:
LUCRETIA:
Child, perhaps
It may be granted. We may all survive
To speak of these sorrows as an old-time tale:
Oh, what a thought! It rises in my breast
Like living blood.
source_text:
LUCRETIA:
Child, perhaps
It will be granted. We may all then live
To make these woes a tale for distant years:
Oh, what a thought! It gushes to my heart
Like the warm blood.

text:
A word above all, lovely as the sky,
Lovely that war and all its savage acts must in time be
utterly lost,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night, again and again, with gentle tirelessness
wash this firm world anew, and yet again;
For my enemy is dead, a man as divine as I am is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin—I draw near,
Bend down and lightly touch with my lips the white face in the coffin.
source_text:
Word over all, beautiful as the sky,
Beautiful that war and all its deeds of carnage must in time be
utterly lost,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night incessantly softly
wash again, and ever again, this solid world;
For my enemy is dead, a man divine as myself is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin--I draw near,
Bend down and touch lightly with my lips the white face in the coffin.

text:
Yet once again, lo! the soul, beyond all learning,  
For it has history gathered like husks around the earth,  
For it the whole star-myriads wheel and roll through the heavens.
source_text:
Yet again, lo! the soul, above all science,
For it has history gather’d like husks around the globe,
For it the entire star-myriads roll through the sky.

text:
Their moss had rotted from them, flake by flake,
Till the stout stalk stood like a murderer’s stake,
Where shreds of loosened flesh still quiver aloft,
And taint the winds that drift along and waft.
source_text:
Their moss rotted off them, flake by flake,
Till the thick stalk stuck like a murderer’s stake,
Where rags of loose flesh yet tremble on high,
Infecting the winds that wander by.

text:
The heirs of fame that never found its hour
Arose from thrones no mortal mind could frame,
Set deep within the Unseen. Chatterton
Rose wan,—his grave and inward anguish had not
Yet ebbed away from him; Sidney, who fought
And in his fall, and in his life and love
Was nobly gentle, a Spirit pure from taint,
Arose; and Lucan, sanctioned by his death:
Oblivion, as they rose, drew back, a thing rebuked.
source_text:
The inheritors of unfulfilled renown
Rose from their thrones, built beyond mortal thought,
Far in the Unapparent. Chatterton
Rose pale,—his solemn agony had not
Yet faded from him; Sidney, as he fought
And as he fell and as he lived and loved
Sublimely mild, a Spirit without spot,
Arose; and Lucan, by his death approved:
Oblivion as they rose shrank like a thing reproved.

text:
Awa wi’ drink! it gies us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it waukens lear,
It steeps us fou o’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony sterner potion,
It ne’er comes short, when drink we deep,
To kittle up our notion,
By night or day.
source_text:
Leeze me on drink! it gies us mair
Than either school or college;
It kindles wit, it waukens lear,
It pangs us fou o’ knowledge:
Be’t whisky-gill or penny wheep,
Or ony stronger potion,
It never fails, or drinkin deep,
To kittle up our notion,
By night or day.

text:
She sweeps with brooms of many hues,
And leaves the shavings spread;
Oh, housewife in the western dusk,
Come back, and dust the pond!
source_text:
She sweeps with many-colored brooms,
And leaves the shreds behind;
Oh, housewife in the evening west,
Come back, and dust the pond!

text:
“Their winding-sheet is bloody clay,
Their graves are turning green to view;
And by them lies the dearest lad
That ever blessed a woman’s eye!
source_text:
“Their winding-sheet the bluidy clay,
Their graves are growin’ green to see;
And by them lies the dearest lad

text:
Did the harebell loose her girdle  
To the lover bee,  
Would the bee the harebell hallow  
As he did before?
source_text:
Did the harebell loose her girdle
To the lover bee,
Would the bee the harebell hallow
Much as formerly?

text:
Once I passed through a thronged city, stamping my mind for future  
use with its spectacles, its buildings, customs, traditions,  
Yet now of all that city I recall only a woman I met by chance  
there who held me back for love of me,  
Day after day and night after night we were together—all else has long  
since faded from my memory,  
I remember, I say, only that woman who clung to me with passion,  
Again we wander, we love, we part again,  
Again she takes my hand, I must not go,  
I see her near beside me with mute lips, sorrowful and trembling.
source_text:
Once I pass’d through a populous city imprinting my brain for future
use with its shows, architecture, customs, traditions,
Yet now of all that city I remember only a woman I casually met
there who detain’d me for love of me,
Day by day and night by night we were together--all else has long
been forgotten by me,
I remember I say only that woman who passionately clung to me,
Again we wander, we love, we separate again,
Again she holds me by the hand, I must not go,
I see her close beside me with silent lips sad and tremulous.

text:
Banner and Pennon:  
Yet louder, loftier, mightier, bard! yet farther, broader rend the air!  
No more let our children think us only treasure and repose,  
We may be dread and slaughter, and so we are today,  
Today we are not any single one of these broad and lordly States, (nor  
any five, nor ten,)  
Nor market-town nor depot are we, nor bank of coin in the city,  
But these and all, and the brown and sweeping land, and the mines  
beneath, are ours,  
And the sea’s dark borders are ours, and the rivers, great and small,  
And the fields they feed, and the grain and the fruit are ours,  
Bays and inlets and ships coming and going are ours--while above all,  
Over the stretch laid out below, the three or four million square  
miles, the capitals,  
The forty million people,--O bard! in life and death sovereign,  
We, yes we, from this hour wave forth our mastery, high overhead,  
Not for the present only, but for a thousand years singing through you,  
This song to the spirit of one poor little child.
source_text:
Banner and Pennant:
Yet louder, higher, stronger, bard! yet farther, wider cleave!
No longer let our children deem us riches and peace alone,
We may be terror and carnage, and are so now,
Not now are we any one of these spacious and haughty States, (nor
any five, nor ten,)
Nor market nor depot we, nor money-bank in the city,
But these and all, and the brown and spreading land, and the mines
below, are ours,
And the shores of the sea are ours, and the rivers great and small,
And the fields they moisten, and the crops and the fruits are ours,
Bays and channels and ships sailing in and out are ours--while we over all,
Over the area spread below, the three or four millions of square
miles, the capitals,
The forty millions of people,--O bard! in life and death supreme,
We, even we, henceforth flaunt out masterful, high up above,
Not for the present alone, for a thousand years chanting through you,
This song to the soul of one poor little child.

text:
CYCLOPS:
What noise is this? No Bacchus is within,
Nor drums, nor ringing castanets of brass.
How fare my little lambs in the cave there?
Are they suckling now, or frisking at their dams?
And has the fresh cheese been set in reed-woven baskets?
Answer! I’ll flog some of you till you weep—
Lift up your eyes, and do not stare at earth.
source_text:
CYCLOPS:
What is this tumult? Bacchus is not here,
Nor tympanies nor brazen castanets.
How are my young lambs in the cavern? Milking
Their dams or playing by their sides? And is
The new cheese pressed into the bulrush baskets?
Speak! I’ll beat some of you till you rain tears—
Look up, not downwards when I speak to you.

text:
IONE:
Ah, my sweet sister,
The tide of sound has slipped away from us,
And you still seem to rise from out its swell,
Because your speech falls like the clear, gentle dew
Shaken from the limbs and tresses of a bathing wood-nymph.
source_text:
IONE:
Ah me! sweet sister,
The stream of sound has ebbed away from us,
And you pretend to rise out of its wave,
Because your words fall like the clear, soft dew
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.

text:
O, to set out to sea in a ship!
To depart this firm, unbearable land,
To leave the weary sameness of the streets, the sidewalks, and the
houses,
To leave you, O you steadfast, motionless land, and board a ship,
To sail and sail and sail!
source_text:
O to sail to sea in a ship!
To leave this steady unendurable land,
To leave the tiresome sameness of the streets, the sidewalks and the
houses,
To leave you O you solid motionless land, and entering a ship,
To sail and sail and sail!

text:
ORSINO:
All shall be well.
Is the petition ready yet? You know
How warmly I pursue whatever you desire, sweet Beatrice;
Have no doubt I’ll use my utmost art
To bring the Pope’s ear to your complaint.
source_text:
ORSINO:
All will be well.
Is the petition yet prepared? You know
My zeal for all you wish, sweet Beatrice;
Doubt not but I will use my utmost skill
So that the Pope attend to your complaint.

text:
Wake the serpent not—lest he
Lose the trail he should pursue,—
Let him creep while still he slumbers
Through the meadow's deep green dew!
Not a bee shall hear him sliding,
Not a may-fly lift or waken
From its bluebell cradle shaken,
Not the starlight, while he passes
Through the grass with quiet gliding.
source_text:
Wake the serpent not—lest he
Should not know the way to go,—
Let him crawl which yet lies sleeping
Through the deep grass of the meadow!
Not a bee shall hear him creeping,
Not a may-fly shall awaken
From its cradling blue-bell shaken,
Not the starlight as he’s sliding
Through the grass with silent gliding.

text:
ORSINO:
There is no need for that. Listen: you know
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s day; he whom your father
Cast down from office? And Marzio,
That reckless man, whom he stripped last year
Of blood’s reward, so well deserved and due?
source_text:
ORSINO:
There is no need of that. Listen: you know
Olimpio, the castellan of Petrella
In old Colonna’s time; him whom your father
Degraded from his post? And Marzio,
That desperate wretch, whom he deprived last year
Of a reward of blood, well earned and due?

text:
Others seek completion, but the Republic is ever building and ever
holds the horizon,
Others praise the vanished past, but you, O days of the present, I praise you,
O days of the future, I trust in you—I set myself apart for your sake,
O America, because you labor for mankind, I labor for you,
O well-loved stone-cutters, I guide those who devise with firmness
and science,
Guide the present with a kindly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which wastes itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, weakness, it is leaving behind.)
source_text:
Others take finish, but the Republic is ever constructive and ever
keeps vista,
Others adorn the past, but you O days of the present, I adorn you,
O days of the future I believe in you--I isolate myself for your sake,
O America because you build for mankind I build for you,
O well-beloved stone-cutters, I lead them who plan with decision
and science,
Lead the present with friendly hand toward the future.
(Bravas to all impulses sending sane children to the next age!
But damn that which spends itself with no thought of the stain,
pains, dismay, feebleness, it is bequeathing.)

text:
PAGE 33. l. 107. flushing. We catch in the sunset’s radiance a  
mirror of the bride’s own blush.
source_text:
PAGE 33. l. 107. blushing. We see in the glow of the sunset a
reflection of the blush of the bride.

text:
Give me to hold all sounds, (I cry, madly wrestling,)
Fill me full of the voices of the universe,
Endow me with their beating, and Nature’s too,
The storms, the waters, winds, operas and chants, marches and dances,
Utter them, pour them in, for I would take them all!
source_text:
Give me to hold all sounds, (I madly struggling cry,)
Fill me with all the voices of the universe,
Endow me with their throbbings, Nature’s also,
The tempests, waters, winds, operas and chants, marches and dances,
Utter, pour in, for I would take them all!

text:
No closed-in room nor school can keep me company,  
But rough men and small children better than they.
source_text:
No shutter’d room or school can commune with me,
But roughs and little children better than they.

text:
Beyond the tugging and the strain there stands what I am,  
There, smiling, self-possessed, compassionate, unoccupied, one,  
Gazes downward, stands straight, or lets an arm rest on some sure invisible support,  
Watching with a head tilted aside, inquisitive what may follow,  
At once within and without the contest, observing and marveling at it.
source_text:
Apart from the pulling and hauling stands what I am,
Stands amused, complacent, compassionating, idle, unitary,
Looks down, is erect, or bends an arm on an impalpable certain rest,
Looking with side-curved head curious what will come next,
Both in and out of the game and watching and wondering at it.

text:
The cold ground lay beneath,  
Above, the cold heavens glowed;  
And all about, in a shivering tone,  
From ice-bound caves and drifts of snow,  
Night’s breath, like death, went drifting low  
Under the sinking moon.
source_text:
The cold earth slept below,
Above the cold sky shone;
And all around, with a chilling sound,
From caves of ice and fields of snow,
The breath of night like death did flow
Beneath the sinking moon.

text:
Time is a trial of grief,  
But not a cure.  
If it should seem to heal, it only proves  
There was no wound.
source_text:
Time is a test of trouble,
But not a remedy.
If such it prove, it prove too
There was no malady.

text:
And where her airy footstep lightly trod,
Her streaming hair upon the grassy sod
Blotted the faint remnant, with a shadowy sweep,
Like a sunny storm above the dark green deep.
source_text:
And wherever her aery footstep trod,
Her trailing hair from the grassy sod
Erased its light vestige, with shadowy sweep,
Like a sunny storm o’er the dark green deep.

text:
His bill is like an auger,  
His head, a cap and frill.  
He labors at each tree, --  
A worm his final aim.
source_text:
His bill an auger is,
His head, a cap and frill.
He laboreth at every tree, --
A worm his utmost goal.

text:
dismal ring  
Of Druid stones, set on a lonely moor,  
When the cold rain starts at fall of dusk,  
In bleak November, and their vaulted chancel,  
The heaven itself is blinded through the night.
source_text:
dismal cirque
Of Druid stones, upon a forlorn moor,
When the chill rain begins at shut of eve,
In dull November, and their chancel vault,
The heaven itself, is blinded throughout night.

text:
It is not to scatter you that you were born of your mother and  
father, it is to name you,  
It is not that you should remain uncertain, but that you should be certain,  
Something long in the making and shapeless has arrived and taken shape in you,  
You are from this hour safe, whatever comes or goes.
source_text:
It is not to diffuse you that you were born of your mother and
father, it is to identify you,
It is not that you should be undecided, but that you should be decided,
Something long preparing and formless is arrived and form’d in you,
You are henceforth secure, whatever comes or goes.

text:
All cloaked and overrun with sly green moss,  
All flecked with tufts of weed,  
The little cage of “Currer Bell”  
In quiet Haworth lay.
source_text:
All overgrown by cunning moss,
All interspersed with weed,
The little cage of 'Currer Bell,'
In quiet Haworth laid.

text: Some methods gather human judgments on a limited set of summaries in order to train an evaluation function. Peyrard et al. (2017) and Peyrard and Gurevych (2018) propose learning such a function from Pyramid and Responsiveness scores by applying standard supervised learning techniques with hand-engineered features. ShafieiBavani et al. (2018b) adopt the same general approach, but construct the features using corpus-based and lexical-resource-based word embeddings. Böhm et al. (2019) train a BERT-based evaluation function using 2,500 human ratings for 500 machine-generated summaries from the CNN/DailyMail dataset; their method shows stronger correlation with human ratings than ROUGE and BLEU. However, because their method is intended for evaluating single-document summaries, it correlates poorly with Pyramid scores for multi-document summaries (see §3).
source_text: Some methods collect human ratings on a small number of summaries to train an evaluation function. Peyrard et al. (2017) ; Peyrard and Gurevych (2018) propose to learn an evaluation function from Pyramid and Responsiveness scores, by using classic supervised learning methods with hand-crafted features. ShafieiBavani et al. (2018b) use the same idea but design corpus based and lexical re-source based word embeddings to build the features. Böhm et al. (2019) train a BERT-based evaluation function with 2,500 human ratings for 500 machinegenerated summaries from the CNN/DailyMail dataset; their method correlates better with human ratings than ROUGE and BLEU. However, as their method is designed for evaluating single-document summaries, it correlates poorly with the Pyramid scores for multi-document summaries (see §3).

text: Although joint models, with or without joint decoding, have been extensively investigated, most prior studies overlook the issue of overlapping relational triples. Zeng et al. (2018) identified three types of overlapping triples and attempted to tackle this problem using a sequence-to-sequence model equipped with a copy mechanism. More recently, Fu et al. (2019) also examined this issue and proposed a method based on graph convolutional networks (GCNs). Despite their preliminary effectiveness, both approaches still regard relations as discrete labels assigned to entity pairs, which makes it rather difficult for the model to learn overlapping triples.
source_text: Though joint models (with or without joint decoding) have been well studied, most previous works ignore the problem of overlapping relational triples. Zeng et al. (2018) introduced three patterns of overlapping triples and try to address the problem via a sequence-to-sequence model with copy mechanism. Recently, Fu et al. (2019) also study the problem and propose a graph convolutional networks (GCNs) based method. Despite their initial success, both methods still treat the relations as discrete labels of entity pairs, making it quite hard for the model to learn overlapping triples.

text: The recent gains in contextual word representations in NLP are largely attributed to newer Transformer-based (Vaswani et al., 2017) models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and XLNet. These Transformer-based models have demonstrated improvements in performance across downstream tasks (Devlin et al., 2018). Nonetheless, their real downstream promise lies in fine-tuning their pre-trained models for specific tasks (Devlin et al., 2018). This is often straightforward for lexical datasets that involve only linguistic modality. However, fine-tuning for multimodal language is neither trivial nor yet well studied, simply because both BERT and XLNet are designed to accept linguistic input only. Therefore, when applying BERT and XLNet to multimodal language, one must either (a) discard the nonverbal information and fine-tune for language, or (b) merely extract word representations and then use a state-of-the-art model for multimodal studies.
source_text: The recent success of contextual word representations in NLP is largely credited to new Transformer-based (Vaswani et al., 2017) models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and XLNet . These Transformer-based models have shown performance improvement across downstream tasks (Devlin et al., 2018) . However, their true downstream potential comes from finetuning their pre-trained models for particular tasks (Devlin et al., 2018) . This is often done easily for lexical datasets which exhibit language modality only. However, this fine-tuning for multimodal language is neither trivial nor yet studied; simply because both BERT and XLNet only expect linguistic input. Therefore, in applying BERT and XLNet to multimodal language, one must either (a) forfeit the nonverbal information and fine-tune for language, or (b) simply extract word representations and proceed to use a state-of-the-art model for multimodal studies.

text: We overcome these limitations by employing contemporary distributed language representations rather than semantic parsers to interpret natural-language explanations. Our method, ExpBERT (Figure 2), uses BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) fine-tuned on the MultiNLI natural language inference dataset (Williams et al., 2018) to generate features that “interpret” each explanation relative to an input. These features are then used to enrich the input representation. In the same way that a semantic parser grounds an explanation by translating it into a logical form and then executing it, the features produced by BERT may be viewed as a soft form of “execution” of the explanation on the input. Across three benchmark relation extraction tasks, ExpBERT outperforms a BERT baseline without explanations: it yields F1 scores 3–10 points higher with the same amount of labeled data, and attains comparable F1 to the full-data baseline while using 3–20× less labeled data. ExpBERT also surpasses a semantic parsing baseline by 3–5 F1 points, indicating that natural-language explanations may be richer than low-level, programmatic ones.
source_text: We remove these limitations by using modern distributed language representations, instead of semantic parsers, to interpret language explanations. Our approach, ExpBERT (Figure 2 ), uses BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) fine-tuned on the MultiNLI natural language inference dataset (Williams et al., 2018) to produce features that "interpret" each explanation on an input. We then use these features to augment the input representation. Just as a semantic parser grounds an explanation by converting it into a logical form and then executing it, the features produced by BERT can be seen as a soft "execution" of the explanation on the input. On three benchmark relation extraction tasks, ExpBERT improves over a BERT baseline with no explanations: it achieves an F1 score of 3-10 points higher with the same amount of labeled data, and a similar F1 score as the full-data baseline but with 3-20x less labeled data. ExpBERT also improves on a semantic parsing baseline (+3 to 5 points F1), suggesting that natural language explanations can be richer than low-level, programmatic explanations.

text: Our work is situated within the research line on massively multilingual translation (Aharoni et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019b). Aharoni et al. (2019) showed that massively multilingual NMT is feasible and reported promising results. We extend this direction by proposing methods that enhance both multilingual and zero-shot performance. Independently of our study, Arivazhagan et al. (2019b) likewise observe that increasing model capacity through deep architectures (Wang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2019) yields substantial gains in multilingual performance. A related concurrent work is Bapna and Firat (2019), which introduces task-specific, lightweight adapters for rapid and scalable model adaptation. In contrast to these adapters, our language-aware layers are trained jointly with the entire NMT model from the outset and do not depend on any pretraining.
source_text: Our work belongs to a line of research on massively multilingual translation (Aharoni et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019b) . Aharoni et al. (2019) demonstrated the feasibility of massively multilingual NMT and reported encouraging results. We continue in this direction by developing approaches that improve both multilingual and zero-shot performance. Independently from our work, Arivazhagan et al. (2019b) also find that increasing model capacity with deep architectures (Wang et al., 2019a; Zhang et al., 2019) substantially improves multilingual performance. A concurrent related work is (Bapna and Firat, 2019) , which introduces taskspecific and lightweight adaptors for fast and scalable model adaptation. Compared to these adaptors, our language-aware layers are jointly trained with the whole NMT model from scratch without relying on any pretraining.

text: The results in Table 4 indicate that: (1) the feedback we gather is genuinely helpful for correcting incorrect parses; (2) incorporating the mispredicted parse facilitates the correction process, since even a simple handcrafted baseline that exploits the mispredicted parses outperforms a strong trained neural model; and (3) the state-of-the-art EditSQL model augmented with BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) attains the best performance, yet it still has difficulty with the task we propose, leaving substantial room for further improvement.
source_text: The results in Table 4 suggest that: (1) the feedback we collect is indeed useful for correcting erroneous parses; (2) incorporating the mispredicted parse helps the correction process (even a simple handcrafted baseline that uses the mispredicted parases outperforms a strong trained neural model); and (3) the state-of-the-art EditSQL model equipped with BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) achieves the best performance, yet it still struggles with the task we introduce, leaving a large gap for improvement.

text: Data augmentation is frequently used to improve robustness in vision (Perez and Wang, 2017) and language (Belinkov and Bisk, 2018; Wei and Zou, 2019), including in NLI (Minervini and Riedel, 2018; Yanaka et al., 2019). In many instances, augmentation with one type of example boosts accuracy on that specific case but fails to transfer to other cases, indicating that models may overfit to the augmentation set (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2018). In particular, McCoy et al. (2019b) showed that augmentation with HANS examples generalized to a different word overlap challenge set (Dasgupta et al., 2018), but only for examples whose lengths were similar to those in HANS. We address such overfitting to superficial characteristics by generating a varied set of corpus-derived examples that differ from the challenge set both lexically and syntactically. Finally, Kim et al. (2018) employed a similar augmentation strategy to ours, but did not examine generalization to example types absent from the augmentation set.
source_text: Data augmentation is often employed to increase robustness in vision (Perez and Wang, 2017) and language (Belinkov and Bisk, 2018; Wei and Zou, 2019) , including in NLI (Minervini and Riedel, 2018; Yanaka et al., 2019) . In many cases, augmentation with one kind of example improves accuracy on that particular case, but does not generalize to other cases, suggesting that models overfit to the augmentation set (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2018; . In particular, McCoy et al. (2019b) found that augmentation with HANS examples generalized to a different word overlap challenge set (Dasgupta et al., 2018) , but only for examples similar in length to HANS examples. We mitigate such overfitting to superficial properties by generating a diverse set of corpus-based examples, which differ from the challenge set both lexically and syntactically. Finally, Kim et al. (2018) used a similar augmentation approach to ours but did not study generalization to types of examples not in the augmentation set.

text: The rationale for the experiments reported in Table 1 is as follows: we extracted word embeddings from pre-trained BERT and XLNet models and used those embeddings to train the baseline models. Because BERT and XLNet are often regarded as yielding superior word embeddings to GloVe, it would be unfair to compare MAG-BERT/MAG-XLNet with prior models trained on GloVe embeddings. We therefore retrain earlier work using these embeddings. Table 1 presents sentiment prediction results on the CMU-MOSI dataset, with the best results highlighted in bold. MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet achieve better performance than the baselines and their language-only fine-tuned counterpart. BA denotes binary accuracy (higher is better, as is F1), MAE denotes mean absolute error (lower is better), and Corr denotes Pearson correlation (higher is better). For BA and F1, we report two values: the number to the left of “/” is computed following Zadeh et al. (2018c), and the number to the right is computed following Tsai et al. (2019). Human performance on CMU-MOSI is reported in Zadeh et al. (2018a). Table 2 reports results for variations of the XLNet model, including MAG applied at different layers of XLNet, as well as input-level concatenation and addition of all modalities. “E” denotes applying MAG immediately after the embedding layer of XLNet, whereas “A” denotes applying MAG after the embedding layer and all subsequent encoding layers. ⊕ and ⊙ denote input-level addition and concatenation of all modalities, respectively. Overall, applying MAG at the initial layers performs better.
source_text: The motivation behind the experiments reported in Table 1 is as follows: we extracted word embeddings from pre-trained BERT and XLNet models and trained the baseline models using those embeddings. Since BERT and XLNet are often perceived to provide better word embeddings than Glove, it is not fair to compare MAG-BERT/MAG-XLNet with previous models trained with Glove embeddings. Therefore, we retrain previous works us- Table 1 : Sentiment prediction results on CMU-MOSI dataset. Best results are highlighted in bold. MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet achieve superior performance than the baselines and their language-only finetuned counterpart. BA denotes binary accuracy (higher is better, same for F1), MAE denotes Mean-absolute Error (lower is better), and Corr is Pearson Correlation (higher is better). For BA and F1, we report two numbers: the number on the left side of "/" is measures calculated based on (Zadeh et al., 2018c) and the right side is measures calculated based on (Tsai et al., 2019) . Human performance for CMU-MOSI is reported as (Zadeh et al., 2018a) . Table 2 : Results of variations of XLNet model: MAG applied at different layers of the XLNet model, inputlevel concatenation and addition of all modalities. "E" denotes application of MAG immediately after embedding layer of the XLNet and "A" denotes applying MAG after the embedding layer and all the subsequent Encoding layers. ⊕ and ⊙ denote input-level addition and concatenation of all modalities respectively. MAG applied at initial layers performs better overall.

text: lines in length and include non-trivial state manipulation. Representative examples are the Magic the Gathering and Hearthstone datasets (Ling et al., 2016), derived from trading cards and Java or Python classes that implement their behavior within a game engine, the CONCODE dataset (Iyer et al., 2018), which consists of Java documentation strings and method bodies, and the NAPS and SPoC datasets (Zavershynskyi et al., 2018; Kulal et al., 2019), which contain pseudocode annotations and source code for programming competition problems. Prior methods for these large-scale language-to-code tasks have generally used sequence-based models (Ling et al., 2016) that do not capture structure on the output side, or tree-based models (Allamanis et al., 2015; Rabinovich et al., 2017a; Yin and Neubig, 2017; Hayati et al., 2018; Iyer et al., 2019) that model syntax but not the semantics of the output domain. Nevertheless, if the goal is to generate programs that can execute correctly, it is essential to incorporate both syntactic and semantic constraints. As illustrated in Figure 1, although several program fragments may be syntactically valid and appear to be plausible translations of the corresponding pseudocode, not all of them will result in executable programs.
source_text: lines in length and including non-trivial state manipulation. Examples include the Magic the Gathering and Hearthstone datasets (Ling et al., 2016) derived from trading cards and Java or Python classes implementing their behavior in a game engine, the CONCODE dataset (Iyer et al., 2018) consisting of Java documentation strings and method bodies, and the NAPS and SPoC datasets (Zavershynskyi et al., 2018; Kulal et al., 2019) consisting of pseudocode annotations and source code for programming competition problems. Past approaches to these large-scale languageto-code tasks have typically employed sequencebased models (Ling et al., 2016 ) that do not account for structure on the output side, or tree-based models (Allamanis et al., 2015; Rabinovich et al., 2017a; Yin and Neubig, 2017; Hayati et al., 2018; Iyer et al., 2019) that incorporate the syntax but not the semantics of the output domain. However, if we want to generate programs that can be executed successfully, the inclusion of both syntactic and semantic constraints is crucial. As shown in Figure 1 , while multiple program fragments may be syntactically correct and represent plausible translations of the corresponding pseudocode, not all of them will lead to executable programs.

text: SUMMA (Liepins et al., 2017) is another comparable system that integrates a range of NLP components and supports multiple media types and languages, enabling the simultaneous monitoring of several media broadcasts. SUMMA employs the online clustering algorithm of Aggarwal and Yu (2006) and the extractive summarization algorithm of Almeida and Martins (2013). Unlike NSTM, SUMMA is designed for settings involving continuous multimedia and multilingual data streams, and it generates substantially longer summaries.
source_text: SUMMA (Liepins et al., 2017) is another comparable system which integrates a variety of NLP components and provides support for numerous media and languages, to simultaneously monitor several media broadcasts. SUMMA applies the online clustering algorithm by Aggarwal and Yu (2006) and the extractive summarization algorithm by Almeida and Martins (2013) . In contrast to NSTM, SUMMA focuses on scenarios with continuous multimedia and multilingual data streams and produces much longer summaries.

text: Dataset Characteristics: Tables 2 and 3 summarize the properties of the two existing datasets and our new dataset, and primarily report averaged values across tasks within each dataset. An individual task corresponds to a single ground-truth timeline that a TLS algorithm is intended to emulate. #PubDates denotes the number of days in an article collection A on which at least one article appears. The compression ratio with respect to sentences (“comp. ratio (sents)”) is m divided by the total number of sentences in A, and the compression ratio with respect to dates is l divided by #PubDates. “Avg. date cov” denotes the average coverage of dates in the ground-truth timeline r by the articles in A. This can be measured either using publication dates in A (“published”) or using textual references to dates within the articles in A (“mentioned”). The observation that textual date references generally cover more ground-truth dates than publication dates indicates that such date mentions should be exploited.
source_text: Dataset Characteristics: Tables 2 and 3 give an overview of properties of the two existing datasets and our new dataset, and mostly show averaged values over tasks in a dataset. An individual task corresponds to one ground-truth timeline that a TLS algorithm aims to simulate. #P ubDates refers to the number of days in an article collection A on which any articles are published. The compression ratio w.r.t. sentences ("comp. ratio (sents)") is m divided by the total number of sentences in A, and the compression ratio w.r.t dates is l divided by #P ubDates. "Avg. date cov" refers to the average coverage of dates in the ground-truth timeline r by the articles in A. This can be counted by using publication dates in A, ("published"), or by textual date references to dates within articles in A ("mentioned"). The fact that there are generally more ground-truth dates covered in textual date references compared to publication dates suggests making use of these date mentions.

text: Application I: Learning with Crowd Annotations. When learning from crowd-sourced data, we treat each worker as an imperfect annotator \(S^{(k)}\) that may commit errors or omit sentences in its annotations. In this setting, different annotators label subsets of the same dataset \(X\), so we assume there are no input distribution shifts among \(X^{(k)}\). We also evaluate only on sentences from the same domain, such that the distribution in the target corpus \(T\) remains unchanged as well. In other words, the marginal distribution of inputs in the target corpus \(P_T(x)\) is identical to that of each individual source dataset, i.e., \(P_T(x) = P_k(x)\). However, because the annotations in each source are imperfect, \(P_k(y \mid x)\) is shifted away from the underlying truth \(P(y \mid x)\) (as illustrated in the top-left part of Fig. 1). The multi-source learning objective in this case is to learn a model \(P_T(y \mid x)\) that supports inference on any new sentence in the same domain.
source_text: Application I: Learning with Crowd Annotations. When learning with crowd-sourced data, we regard each worker as an imperfect annotator (S (k) ), who may make mistakes or skip sentences in its annotations. Note that in this setting, different annotators tag subsets of the same given dataset (X ), and thus we assume there are no input distribution shifts among X (k) . Also, we only test sentences in the same domain such that the distribution in target corpus T is the same as well. That is, the marginal distribution of target corpus P T (x) is the same with that for each individual source dataset, i.e. P T (x) = P k (x). However, due to imperfectness of the annotations in each source, P k (y|x) is shifted from the underlying truth P (y|x) (illustrated in the top-left part of Fig. 1 ). The multi-source learning objective here is to learn a model P T (y|x) for supporting infer-ence on any new sentences in the same domain.

text: Algorithm 1: BPE-dropout. Initialize current split as the characters of the input word; set merges to all possible merges of tokens in the current split; for each merge in merges do /* The only difference from BPE */ remove the merge from merges with probability p; end if. If merges is not empty, select the merge with the highest priority from merges and apply it to the current split; continue while merges is not empty; return the current split.
source_text: Algorithm 1: BPE-dropout current split ← characters from input word; do merges ← all possible merges 1 of tokens from current split; for merge from merges do / * The only difference from BPE * / remove merge from merges with the probability p; end if merges is not empty then merge ← select the merge with the highest priority from merges; apply merge to current split; end while merges is not empty; return current split;

text: Q2: How well does the self-supervised metric align with human judgments? Datasets. To train MAUDE, we use PersonaChat (Zhang et al., 2018), a large-scale open-domain chit-chat dataset collected from human-human conversations based on provided user personas. We extract and process the data with the ParlAI (Miller et al.) platform. For training and validation, we use the public train split, and for testing we use the public validation split. For correlation analysis, we use the human-human and human-model data collected by See et al. (2019), in which the models themselves are trained on PersonaChat. Baselines. As LSTM-based baselines, we employ InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017) and unreferenced RUBER. We also compare with BERT-NLI, which is identical to the InferSent model except that the LSTM encoder is replaced by a pretrained BERT encoder. These baselines can be regarded as ablations of the MAUDE framework with simplified text encoders, since we apply the same NCE training loss to ensure a fair comparison. In addition, note that in practice we use DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) rather than BERT in both MAUDE and the BERT-NLI baseline, and therefore we refer to the BERT-NLI baseline as DistilBERT-NLI. 1 .
source_text: • Q2: How well does the self-supervised metric correlate with human judgements? Datasets. For training MAUDE, we use Per-sonaChat (Zhang et al., 2018) , a large-scale opendomain chit-chat style dataset which is collected by human-human conversations over provided user persona. We extract and process the dataset using ParlAI (Miller et al.) platform. We use the public train split for our training and validation, and the public validation split for testing. We use the human-human and human-model data collected by See et al. (2019) for correlation analysis, where the models themselves are trained on PersonaChat. Baselines. We use InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017) and unreferenced RUBER as LSTM-based baselines. We also compare against BERT-NLI, which is the same as the InferSent model but with the LSTM encoder replaced with a pre-trained BERT encoder. Note that these baselines can be viewed as ablations of the MAUDE framework using simplified text encoders, since we use the same NCE training loss to provide a fair comparison. Also, note that in practice, we use DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) instead of BERT in both MAUDE and the BERT-NLI baseline (and thus we refer to the BERT-NLI baseline as DistilBERT-NLI). 1 .

text: Since the initial Seq2Seq model of Du et al. (2017), numerous enhancements have been proposed: introducing different methods for encoding answer information, thereby enabling higher-quality answer-focused question generation (Sun et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019); improving training by combining supervised learning with reinforcement learning to optimize question-specific rewards (Yuan et al., 2017); and integrating a range of linguistic features into the QG process (Liu et al., 2019a). Nevertheless, these methods address only sentence-level QG. By contrast, our work focuses on the problem of generating deep questions that require multi-hop reasoning over document-level contexts.
source_text: Many improvements have been proposed since the first Seq2Seq model of Du et al. (2017) : applying various techniques to encode the answer information, thus allowing for better quality answerfocused questions Sun et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019) ; improving the training via combining supervised and reinforcement learning to maximize question-specific rewards (Yuan et al., 2017) ; and incorporating various linguistic features into the QG process (Liu et al., 2019a) . However, these approaches only consider sentence-level QG. In contrast, our work focus on the challenge of generating deep questions with multi-hop reasoning over document-level contexts.

text: Model-based or model-free text generation aims to produce the next word (action) conditioned on the current (sub-)sentence (state). The generator is treated as an agent that learns a policy for predicting the next word from its current state. In prior work (Ranzato et al., 2016), a metric reward is assigned and the generator is trained solely to maximize this reward through trial, and this therefore constitutes model-free learning. In the proposed approach, the guider network captures the environment dynamics and is trained by minimizing the cosine similarity between its prediction and the ground truth on real text. During generator training, the model maximizes a reward determined by both the metric and the guider network, and thus the method is model-free learning with model-based boosting (Gu et al., 2016). Our method also incorporates a model predictive control scheme, in which the guider network assists next-word selection at each time step.
source_text: Model-based or Model-free Text generation seeks to generate the next word (action) given the current (sub-)sentence (state). The generator is considered as an agent that learns a policy to predict the next word given its current state. In previous work (Ranzato et al., 2016) , a metric reward is given and the generator is trained to only maximize the metric reward by trial, thus this is model-free learning. In the proposed method, the guider network models the environment dynamics, and is trained by minimizing the cosine similarity between the prediction and the ground truth on real text. For generator training, it maximizes the reward which is determined by the metric and guider network, and thus is model-free learning with model-based boosting (Gu et al., 2016) . The model predictive control scheme is included in our method, where the guider network is used to help next-word selection at each time-step.

text: Training data creation details are provided in §§4.2 and 4.3. Figure 3 presents the overall pipeline of the proposed method. In the first example x 11, where no explicit style attribute is apparent, our model inserts the [TAG] token into z(x 1), thereby indicating that a target-style marker should be produced at that position. By contrast, in the second example, the words “ok” and “bland” serve as negative-sentiment markers, and the tagger accordingly replaces them with [TAG] tokens in z(x 2). As can be observed, the inferred sentence in both cases is devoid of the original and target styles. The structural bias introduced by this two-stage design is useful for producing an interpretable style-free tagged sentence that explicitly represents the content. In the sections that follow, we describe in detail the methods used for (1) estimating the relevant attribute markers for a given style, (2) the tagger, and (3) the generator modules of our approach.
source_text: tokens. Training data creation details are given in sections §4.2, §4.3. Fig. 3 shows the overall pipeline of the proposed approach. In the first example x 11 , where there is no clear style attribute present, our model adds the [TAG] token in z(x 1 ), indicating that a target style marker should be generated in this position. On the contrary, in the second example, the terms "ok" and "bland" are markers of negative sentiment and hence the tagger has replaced them with [TAG] tokens in z(x 2 ). We can also see that the inferred sentence in both the cases is free of the original and target styles. The structural bias induced by this two staged approach is helpful in realizing an interpretable style free tagged sentence that explicitly encodes the content. In the following sections we discuss in detail the methodologies involved in (1) estimating the relevant attribute markers for a given style, (2) tagger, and (3) generator modules of our approach.
