text: Adversarial learning Adversarial attacks aim to deceive neural models by presenting them with adversarial examples (Szegedy et al., 2014). In a white-box attack setting (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Ebrahimi et al., 2018), we assume that the attacker has access to the model parameters, in contrast to the black-box setting (Alzantot et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2018), in which the attacker can only sample model predictions for given examples. Adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2017; Yasunaga et al., 2018), by contrast, seeks to enhance the robustness of neural models by incorporating adversarial examples during training.
source_text: Adversarial learning Adversarial attacks seek to mislead the neural models by feeding them with adversarial examples (Szegedy et al., 2014) . In a white-box attack scenario (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Ebrahimi et al., 2018) we assume that the attacker has access to the model parameters, in contrast to the black-box scenario (Alzantot et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2018) , where the attacker can only sample model predictions on given examples. Adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2017; Yasunaga et al., 2018) , on the other hand, aims to improve the robustness of the neural models by utilizing adversarial examples during training.

text: Beginning from the initial state s 0, a recurrent neural network (RNN) generates a sequence of states (s 1, . . . , s T ) from an input sentence-feature representation (e(y 1), . . . , e(y T )), where e(•) denotes a word embedding function that maps a token to its d-dimensional feature representation. The states are then updated recursively by a function referred to as the cell: s t = h(s t−1, e(y t )). At position t, one usually assigns the following probability to an observation y: p(y|Y <t ) = [softmax(g(s t ))] y . Taken together, g and h define a probabilistic model π, that is, log π(Y ) = t log p(y t |Y <t ).
source_text: Starting from the initial state s 0 , a recurrent neural network (RNN) produces a sequence of states (s 1 , . . . , s T ) given an input sentence-feature representation (e(y 1 ), . . . , e(y T )), where e(•) denotes a word embedding function mapping a token to its ddimensional feature representation. The states are recursively updated with a function known as the cell: s t = h(s t−1 , e(y t )). One typically assigns the following probability to an observation y at location t: p(y|Y <t ) = [softmax(g(s t ))] y . Together (g, h) specifies a probabilistic model π, i.e., log π(Y ) = t log p(y t |Y <t ).

text: There are three strands of related work connected to this paper: sequence labeling, crowdsourcing, and unsupervised domain adaptation. Neural Sequence Labeling. Conventional sequence-labeling methods typically require substantial feature-engineering effort for graphical models such as conditional random fields (CRFs) (Lafferty, 2001). More recent work with neural network models has demonstrated that end-to-end learning, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) (Ma and Hovy, 2016a) or bidirectional long short-term memory networks (BLSTMs) (Lample et al., 2016), can greatly reduce the need for human-crafted features.
source_text: There exists three threads of related work with this paper, which are sequence labeling, crowdsourcing and unsupervised domain adaptation. Neural Sequence Labeling. Traditional approaches for sequence labeling usually need significant efforts in feature engineering for graphical models like conditional random fields (CRFs) (Lafferty, 2001) . Recent research efforts in neural network models have shown that end-to-end learning like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) (Ma and Hovy, 2016a) or bidirectional long short-term memory (BLSTMs) (Lample et al., 2016) can largely eliminate humancrafted features.

text: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) Recent studies (Anderson et al., 2018; Thomason et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019; Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019) build upon earlier instruction-based navigation work (Chen and Mooney, 2011; Kim and Mooney, 2013; Mei et al., 2016) in photorealistic simulated settings. For example, Anderson et al. (2018) proposed learning a multimodal Sequence-to-Sequence agent (Seq2Seq) through imitation of expert demonstrations. Fried et al. (2018) introduced an approach that expands the paired instruction-demonstration training data with a learned speaker model, so as to help the navigation agent more effectively interpret instructions. Wang et al. (2019) further employed reinforcement learning (RL) and self-imitation learning to enhance navigation agents. Ma et al. (2019a,b) developed models that use soft attention to monitor execution progress across a sequence of instructions.
source_text: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) Recent works (Anderson et al., 2018; Thomason et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019; Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019) extend the early works of instruction based navigation (Chen and Mooney, 2011; Kim and Mooney, 2013; Mei et al., 2016) to photo-realistic simulated environments. For instance, Anderson et al. (2018) proposed to learn a multi-modal Sequence-to-Sequence agent (Seq2Seq) by imitating expert demonstration. Fried et al. (2018) developed a method that augments the paired instruction and demonstration data using a learned speaker model, to teach the navigation agent to better understand instructions. Wang et al. (2019) further applies reinforcement learning (RL) and self-imitation learning to improve navigation agents. Ma et al. (2019a,b) designed models that track the execution progress for a sequence of instructions using soft-attention.

text: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation. Unsupervised cross-domain adaptation seeks to transfer knowledge learned from high-resource source domains to improve performance on low-resource target domains of interest, such as social media messages (Lin et al., 2017). Unlike supervised adaptation (Lin and Lu, 2018), we assume that there are no labels at all for the target corpora. Saito et al. (2017) and Ruder and Plank (2018) investigated bootstrapping with a multitask tri-training approach, which requires unlabeled data from the target domain. This method is designed for one-to-one domain adaptation and does not capture differences among multiple source domains. Yang and Eisenstein (2015) represent each domain by a vector of metadata domain attributes and use domain vectors to train the model to handle domain shift, but this approach is highly dependent on prior domain knowledge. Ghifary et al. (2016) employ an autoencoder-based method by jointly training a predictor for source labels and a decoder to reconstruct target input using a shared encoder. The decoder serves as a normalizer that compels the model to learn knowledge shared between the source and target domains. An adversarial penalty can be added to the loss function to encourage models to learn only domain-invariant features (Fernando et al., 2015; Long et al., 2014; Ming Harry Hsu et al., 2015). However, this does not exploit domain-specific information.
source_text: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation. Unsupervised cross-domain adaptation aims to transfer knowledge learned from high-resource domains (source domains) to boost performance on lowresource domains (target domains) of interests such as social media messages (Lin et al., 2017) . Different from supervised adaptation (Lin and Lu, 2018), we assume there is no labels at all for target corpora. Saito et al. (2017) and Ruder and Plank (2018) explored bootstrapping with multitask tri-training approach, which requires unlabeled data from the target domain. The method is developed for one-to-one domain adaptation and does not model the differences among multiple source domains. Yang and Eisenstein (2015) represents each domain with a vector of metadata domain attributes and uses domain vectors to train the model to deal with domain shifting, which is highly dependent on prior domain knowledge. (Ghifary et al., 2016) uses an auto-encoder method by jointly training a predictor for source labels, and a decoder to reproduce target input with a shared encoder. The decoder acts as a normalizer to force the model to learn shared knowledge between source and target domains. Adversarial penalty can be added to the loss function to make models learn domain-invariant feature only (Fernando et al., 2015; Long et al., 2014; Ming Harry Hsu et al., 2015) . However, it does not exploit domain-specific information.

text: Architecture Search for IB-BERT Our design principle for the teacher model is to adopt the smallest possible inter-block hidden size (feature map size), provided that no loss in accuracy occurs. Following this principle, we conduct experiments that vary the inter-block size of a BERT LARGE-sized IB-BERT, and the results are reported in Table 2 under labels (a)–(e). As can be seen, decreasing the inter-block hidden size does not impair BERT’s performance until it falls below 512. Accordingly, we select IB-BERT LARGE with an inter-block hidden size of 512 as the teacher model. One may ask whether the teacher’s intra-block hidden size can also be reduced. We therefore perform additional experiments, whose results are shown in Table 2 under labels (f)–(i). The results indicate that reducing the intra-block hidden size leads to a substantial deterioration in model performance. This suggests that the intra-block hidden size, which captures the representational capacity of the non-linear modules, is critical in BERT. Therefore, unlike the inter-block hidden size, we do not reduce the intra-block hidden size in our teacher model.
source_text: Architecture Search for IB-BERT Our design philosophy for the teacher model is to use as small inter-block hidden size (feature map size) as possible, as long as there is no accuracy loss. Under this guideline, we design experiments to manipulate the inter-block size of a BERT LARGE -sized IB-BERT, and the results are shown in Table 2 with labels (a)-(e). We can see that reducing the interblock hidden size doesn't damage the performance of BERT until it is smaller than 512. Hence, we choose IB-BERT LARGE with its inter-block hidden size being 512 as the teacher model. One may wonder whether we can also shrink the intra-block hidden size of the teacher. We conduct experiments and the results are shown in Table 2 with labels (f)-(i). We can see that when the intra-block hidden size is reduced, the model performance is dramatically worse. This means that the intra-block hidden size, which represents the representation power of non-linear modules, plays a crucial role in BERT. Therefore, unlike the interblock hidden size, we do not shrink the intra-block hidden size of our teacher model.

text: Our work is related to denoising autoencoders (DAEs; Vincent et al., 2008), which have been successfully employed as unsupervised approaches across a range of NLP tasks. Prior studies have demonstrated that DAEs can be used to learn high-level text representations for domain adaptation (Glorot et al., 2011) as well as multimodal representations of textual and visual inputs (Silberer and Lapata, 2014). More recent research has extended DAEs to text generation, in particular data-to-text generation (Freitag and Roy, 2018) and extractive sentence compression (Fevry and Phang, 2018). Our model departs from these approaches in two ways. First, whereas earlier work has relied on simplistic noising strategies such as randomly inserting or deleting words (Fevry and Phang, 2018) and randomly perturbing encodings (Silberer and Lapata, 2014), our noise generators are more linguistically motivated and better suited to the opinion summarization task. Second, although Freitag and Roy (2018) restrict the decoder to standard RNNs, our noising procedure allows the use of more sophisticated architectures augmented with attention and copy mechanisms, which have been shown to improve summarization performance (Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017).
source_text: Our work relates to denoising autoencoders (DAEs; Vincent et al., 2008) , which have been effectively used as unsupervised methods for various NLP tasks. Earlier approaches have shown that DAEs can be used to learn high-level text representations for domain adaptation (Glorot et al., 2011) and multimodal representations of textual and visual input (Silberer and Lapata, 2014) . Recent work has applied DAEs to text generation tasks, specifically to data-to-text generation (Freitag and Roy, 2018) and extractive sentence compression (Fevry and Phang, 2018) . Our model differs from these approaches in two respects. Firstly, while previous work has adopted trivial noising methods such as randomly adding or removing words (Fevry and Phang, 2018) and randomly corrupting encodings (Silberer and Lapata, 2014) , our noise generators are more linguistically informed and suitable for the opinion summarization task. Secondly, while in Freitag and Roy (2018) the decoder is limited to vanilla RNNs, our noising method enables the use of more complex architectures, enhanced with attention and copy mechanisms, which are known to improve the performance of summarization systems (Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017) .

text: To estimate how many HIGH RCs were required in training for a HIGH bias to emerge, we first forced all RC attachment in the training set to be HIGH attachment. We then varied, in increments of 10 RC sentences at a time, the proportion of RC sentences relative to filler sentences during training. For each training condition, that is, each RC proportion, we trained 5 RNNs. This experiment yielded a lower bound on the number of HIGH RCs necessary in the training data to overcome any recency bias in the RNNs when every RC displayed HIGH attachment. When only 0.017% of the data, corresponding to 20 sentences, contained RCs with HIGH attachment, the test-set surprisal difference between HIGH and LOW attachment differed significantly from zero (p < 10−5, BayesFactor (BF) > 100),10 with a negative mean difference (µ = −2.24). These findings suggest that the models could learn a HIGH bias from just 20/120000 examples of HIGH RC attachment.
source_text: To determine how many HIGH RCs were needed in training to learn a HIGH bias, we first constrained all the RC attachment in the training data to HIGH attachment. Then, we varied the proportion (in increments of 10 RC sentences at a time) of RC sentences to filler sentences during training. We trained 5 RNNs for each training configuration (i.e. each proportion of RCs). This experiment provided a lower bound on the number of HIGH RCs needed in the training data to overcome any RNN recency bias when all RCs exhibited HIGH attachment. When as little as 0.017% (20 sentences) of the data contained RCs with HIGH attachment, the test difference in surprisal between HIGH and LOW attachment significantly differed from zero (p < 10 −5 , BayesFactor (BF) > 100), 10 with a mean difference less than zero (µ = −2.24). These results indicate that the models were able to acquire a HIGH bias with only 20/120000 examples of HIGH RC attachment.

text: Secondly, several categories are reserved exclusively for particular backchannels. Backchannels are brief utterances that interrupt longer turns produced by another conversational participant (Yngve, 1970; Goodwin, 1986; Bavelas et al., 2000). In particular, the model assigns the backchannels “mhm,” “mm,” “nice,” and “awesome” to four separate categories. Prior research indicates that it is indeed beneficial to examine the effects of different backchannel types separately. For instance, Bavelas et al. (2000) distinguish between specific backchannels, such as “nice” and “awesome,” and generic backchannels, such as “mm” and “mhm,” and Tolins and Fox Tree (2014) showed that each backchannel type fulfills a different role in conversation.
source_text: Secondly, there are several categories reserved exclusively for certain backchannels. Backchannels are short utterances that punctuate longer turns by another conversational participant (Yngve, 1970; Goodwin, 1986; Bavelas et al., 2000) . Specifically, the model assigns the backchannels "mhm," "mm," "nice," and "awesome" each to separate categories. Research shows that it is indeed useful to consider the effects different types of backchannels separately. For example, Bavelas et al. (2000) propose a distinction between specific backchannels (such as "nice" and "awesome") and generic backchannels (such as "mm" and "mhm"), and Tolins and Fox Tree (2014) demonstrated that each backchannel type serves a different purpose in conversation.

text: We examine the use of importance sampling for evaluating latent language models. Our contributions are fourfold: (1) we demonstrate that importance sampling yields stochastic upper bounds on perplexity, thereby supporting the use of such estimates when comparing language model performance; (2) we provide a brief account of common, though sometimes implicit, practices employed in applying this method; (3) we present a straightforward alternative based on direct marginalization rather than importance sampling; and (4) we report experimental findings that show how sample size, sampling distribution, and granularity influence the resulting estimates.
source_text: We investigate the application of importance sampling to evaluating latent language models. Our contributions include: (1) showing that importance sampling produces stochastic upper bounds of perplexity, thereby justifying the use of such estimates for comparing language model performance, (2) a concise description of (sometimes unstated) common practices used in applying this technique, (3) a simple direct marginalization-based alternative to importance sampling, and (4) experimental results demonstrating the effect of sample size, sampling distribution, and granularity on estimates.

text: To assess LP models on KGs, evaluation facts are created by sampling from the KG. Given an evaluation triple (i, k, j), the most straightforward way to prevent leakage from the training set is to remove only that evaluation triple from training. However, for KGs, it has been shown that this naïve strategy is insufficient, because evaluation answers may still leak and therefore be inferred directly (Toutanova et al., 2015; Dettmers et al., 2018). For instance, an evaluation triple (a, siblingOf, b) may be trivially answered using the training triple (b, siblingOf, a).
source_text: To evaluate LP models for KGs, evaluation facts are generated by sampling from the KG. Given an evaluation triple (i, k, j), the simplest action to avoid leakage from the training data is to remove only this evaluation triple from training. For KGs, it was observed this simple approach is not satisfactory in that evaluation answers may still leak and thus can be trivially inferred (Toutanova et al., 2015; Dettmers et al., 2018) . For example, an evaluation triple (a, siblingOf, b) can be trivially answered with the training triple (b, siblingOf, a).

text: A substantial body of work lies at the intersection of natural language processing (NLP), psycholinguistics, and clinical psychology. For instance, researchers have examined correlations between counselor–patient interaction dynamics and counseling outcomes (Althoff et al., 2016); investigated the linguistic development of mental healthcare counsellors (Zhang et al., 2019); identified differences in how individuals disclose mental illnesses across gender and culture (De Choudhury et al., 2017); predicted a range of mental health conditions from social media posts (Sekulic and Strube, 2019; De Choudhury et al., 2013a; Guntuku et al., 2019; Coppersmith et al., 2014); and analyzed well-being (Smith et al., 2016) and distress (Buechel et al., 2018).
source_text: Much work exists at the intersection of natural language processing (NLP), psycholinguistics, and clinical psychology. For example, exploring correlations between counselor-patient interaction dynamics and counseling outcomes (Althoff et al., 2016) ; studying linguistic development of mental healthcare counsellors (Zhang et al., 2019) ; identifying differences in how people disclose mental illnesses across gender and culture (De Choudhury et al., 2017) ; predicting a variety of mental health conditions from social media posts (Sekulic and Strube, 2019; De Choudhury et al., 2013a; Guntuku et al., 2019; Coppersmith et al., 2014) ; and analyzing well-being (Smith et al., 2016) and distress (Buechel et al., 2018) .

For intrinsic hallucinations (the second column in Table 2) , GOLD is significantly different from all other systems. text: Using the P/Q graph. At the outset of a conversation, no question is answered because no paragraph has yet been presented to the user. Accordingly, the system begins with an empty P/Q graph (the left graph in Figure 3). As the system discloses paragraphs during the conversation, these paragraphs are designated as read in the P/Q graph (the shaded blue paragraphs in the right graph of Figure 3). Under our Q&A model, any question linked to a read paragraph is considered answered, so we label all neighbors of read paragraphs as answered questions (the shaded blue questions in the right graph of Figure 3). At any point in the conversation, if a paragraph is connected only to answered questions, it is regarded as uninformative, since it cannot provide the answer to any new question.
source_text: Using the P/Q graph. At the start of a conversation, no question is answered, since no paragraph has been shown to the user. Therefore, the system initializes a blank P/Q graph (left graph in Figure 3 ). As the system reveals paragraphs in the conversation, they are marked as read in the P/Q graph (shaded blue paragraphs in the right graph of Figure 3) . According to our Q&A model, any question connected to a read paragraph is answered, so we mark all neighbors of read paragraphs as answered questions (shaded blue questions on the right graph of Figure 3) . At any stage in the conversation, if a paragraph is connected to only answered questions, it is deemed uninformative, as it will not reveal the answer to a new question.

text: A natural way to enhance capacity in Deep Transformer One is to increase model depth. Deeper neural models are often able to learn more generalizable (“abstract”) representations and to model more complex dependencies, and they have demonstrated promising performance in bilingual translation (Bapna et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a). To train a deep Transformer for multilingual translation, we employ the depth-scaled initialization method (Zhang et al., 2019).
source_text: Deep Transformer One natural way to improve the capacity is to increase model depth. Deeper neural models are often capable of inducing more generalizable ('abstract') representations and capturing more complex dependencies and have shown encouraging performance on bilingual translation (Bapna et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019a) . We adopt the depth-scaled initialization method (Zhang et al., 2019) to train a deep Transformer for multilingual translation.

text: Recently, pretrained Transformer networks have achieved strong results on a range of NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019); we adopt these models as the basis of SPECTER. In particular, we use SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), an adaptation of the original BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) architecture for the scientific domain. The BERT model architecture (Devlin et al., 2019) employs multiple layers of Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) to encode the tokens in a given input sequence. Each layer comprises a self-attention sublayer followed by a feedforward sublayer. The final hidden state corresponding to the special [CLS] token is typically referred to as the “pooled output” and is widely used as an aggregate representation of the sequence.
source_text: Recently, pretrained Transformer networks have demonstrated success on various NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019) ; we use these models as the foundation for SPECTER. Specifically, we use SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) which is an adaptation of the original BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) architecture to the scientific domain. The BERT model architecture (Devlin et al., 2019) uses multiple layers of Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) to encode the tokens in a given input sequence. Each layer consists of a self-attention sublayer followed by a feedforward sublayer. The final hidden state associated with the special [CLS] token is usually called the "pooled output", and is commonly used as an aggregate representation of the sequence.

text: The model must consider O(n 4 ) possible span combinations, where n denotes the number of words in a sentence. Consequently, pruning is necessary to make the assignment of relation labels to span pairs computationally feasible during both training and inference (Lee et al., 2017; He et al., 2018). First, a mention score φ mr is calculated to estimate how likely a span is to participate in a relation. These mention scores are then used for beam pruning to restrict the set of spans considered for relation classification, with a variable beam of size λ n , where n is the number of tokens in the input sentence (Luan et al., 2018a). Second, the maximum span width W is constrained in order to further reduce the total number of spans. We set λ to .8 and W to 14 tokens, which is the maximum span length in FOBIE.
source_text: The model deals with O(n 4 ) possible combinations of spans, where n is the number of words in a sentence. Therefore, pruning is required to make the classification of span-pairs into relation labels tractable at both training and test time (Lee et al., 2017; He et al., 2018) . First, a score φ mr of how likely a span is mentioned in a relation is computed. These mention scores enable beam pruning the number of spans considered for relation classification with a variable beam of size λ n , where n is the number of tokens in the input sentence (Luan et al., 2018a) . Second, the maximum width W of spans is limited to reduce the total number of spans. We set λ to .8 and W to 14 tokens, the maximum span length in FOBIE.

text: where s is defined in Section 3.1. As can be observed, when µ is large, the Bregman divergence at each iteration of the VBPP method effectively acts as a strong regularizer, preventing θ t+1 from deviating substantially from the preceding iterate θ t. In the optimization literature, this is also referred to as a trust-region type iteration (Conn et al., 2000). As a result, the Bregman proximal point method can preserve the knowledge from the out-of-domain data contained in the pre-trained model f (•; θ 0 ). Because each subproblem (2) in VBPP does not have a closed-form solution, it must be solved with SGD-type algorithms such as ADAM. We note that it is unnecessary to solve every subproblem to convergence; only a small number of iterations is sufficient to produce a reliable initial solution for the subsequent subproblem.
source_text: , where s is defined in Section 3.1. As can be seen, when µ is large, the Bregman divergence at each iteration of the VBPP method essentially serves as a strong regularizer and prevents θ t+1 from deviating too much from the previous iterate θ t . This is also known as the trust-region type iteration in existing optimization literature (Conn et al., 2000) . Consequently, the Bregman proximal point method can effectively retain the knowledge of the out-of-domain data in the pre-trained model f (•; θ 0 ). Since each subproblem (2) of VBPP does not admit a closed-form solution, we need to solve it using SGD-type algorithms such as ADAM. Note that we do not need to solve each subproblem until convergence. A small number of iterations are sufficient to output a reliable initial solution for solving the next subproblem.

text: In recent years, deep learning methods have become highly popular in Natural Language Processing (NLP), for example, because they achieve strong performance by using very simple input representations (e.g., Kim, 2014; Goldberg, 2016; Kim et al., 2016). In particular, Transformer-based architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) produce input representations as the outcome of a pre-training phase. These models are trained on large-scale corpora and then effectively fine-tuned on a target task, thereby obtaining state-of-the-art results across diverse and heterogeneous NLP tasks. Such gains are typically realized when thousands of annotated examples are available for the final task. As observed in this work, however, the performance of BERT fine-tuned on fewer than 200 annotated instances drops substantially, especially in classification tasks with many categories. Unfortunately, producing annotated data is time-consuming and expensive. A plausible solution is to adopt semi-supervised methods, such as those in Weston et al. (2008), Chapelle et al. (2010), Yang et al. (2016), and Kipf and Welling (2016), to enhance generalization when only a small amount of annotated data is available but unlabeled sources can be acquired.
source_text: In recent years, Deep Learning methods have become very popular in Natural Language Processing (NLP), e.g., they reach high performances by relying on very simple input representations (for example, in (Kim, 2014; Goldberg, 2016; Kim et al., 2016) ). In particular, Transformer-based architectures, e.g., BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , provide representations of their inputs as a result of a pre-training stage. These are, in fact, trained over large scale corpora and then effectively finetuned over a targeted task achieving state-of-the-art results in different and heterogeneous NLP tasks. These achievements are obtained when thousands of annotated examples exist for the final tasks. As experimented in this work, the quality of BERT fine-tuned over less than 200 annotated instances shows significant drops, especially in classification tasks involving many categories. Unfortunately, obtaining annotated data is a time-consuming and costly process. A viable solution is adopting semisupervised methods, such as in (Weston et al., 2008; Chapelle et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2016; Kipf and Welling, 2016) to improve the generalization capability when few annotated data is available, while the acquisition of unlabeled sources is possible.

text: Results: From Table 3, we find that all strategies sharing information across languages perform better (D0-S vs. D0), with soft logits contributing more benefit than hard targets (D1 vs. D0-S). Notably, we observe that merely combining the representation loss with logits (D3.1 vs. D2) degrades the model. This strategy also appears to be sensitive to the hyperparameters (α, β, γ in Eqn. 7) used to balance the multiple loss functions. We vary the hyperparameters in multiples of 10 and report the best results.
source_text: Results: From Table 3 , we observe all strategies that share information across languages to work better (D0-S vs. D0) with soft logits adding more value than hard targets (D1 vs. D0-S). Interestingly, we observe simply combining representation loss with logits (D3.1 vs. D2) hurts the model. We observe this strategy to be vulnerable to the hyperparameters (α, β, γ in Eqn. 7) used to combine multiple loss functions. We vary hyper-parameters in multiples of 10 and report best numbers.

text: To verify the generalizability of our framework, we reimplemented CONNET and several baselines (MTV-SLM, Crowd-Add, and Gold) using Transformer-CRF as the base model. Specifically, the base model uses a Transformer as the encoder for the CRF, which has demonstrated its effectiveness on many NLP tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019). The Transformer models sequences with self-attention and removes all recurrence. Following the experimental settings in (Vaswani et al., 2017), we set the number of heads in multi-head attention to 8, the dimension of the keys and values to 64, and the hidden size of the feed-forward layers to 1024. We conduct experiments on the crowd-annotation dataset AMTC for the NER task and the cross-domain dataset UD for the POS task, as described in Section 5.1. The results are presented in Table 4. As can be observed, our model outperforms the other baselines in both tasks and applications. Fig. 3 and Fig. 7 with NER annotations including PER (red), ORG (blue), LOC (violet), and MISC (orange).
